### Turn 7 — White → Black  <sub>21:52:05</sub>

What is documented about roundabout transitions is that driver error rates typically peak in the first two to six weeks of operation, then decline significantly as familiarity builds. Regarding the three near-misses Black mentioned — we'd need to know whether those were formally reported, because anecdotal observations and official incident records can diverge considerably. We also don't yet have data on what signage or driver education accompanied this rollout, which is a meaningful gap given that research consistently links transition safety to advance public information campaigns.

### Turn 9 — White → Red  <sub>21:52:23</sub>

To add before Yellow continues — what is known is that roundabouts, once adopted, reduce injury-causing collisions by approximately 75% compared to traditional intersections, according to Federal Highway Administration data. That is the baseline Yellow is likely drawing on. One gap worth flagging now: we don't yet know whether this installation meets standard geometric design criteria, which affects whether those long-term safety figures actually apply here.

### Turn 13 — White → Black  <sub>21:53:40</sub>

One data point worth surfacing before we move on: the timeline Black just described — anecdote outpacing data — is actually well-documented in infrastructure rollout research. Studies on public risk perception consistently show that a single vivid near-miss story can shift community confidence more than several weeks of clean safety statistics. That is not speculation; it is a measurable asymmetry in how people process risk information.
